content.text: is the design practice of storing all agent state — session context, task progress, memory, artifacts, and locks — in dedicated external services rather than in the agent process's own memory. The agent process itself becomes

: it can be killed, crashed, restarted, or replaced by an entirely new instance without any data loss, because all data that must survive beyond a single execution step lives outside the process boundary.

In a stateful design, an agent holds its conversation history, tool outputs, and intermediate results in Python dictionaries or object attributes in RAM. The moment the process dies, that data is gone. In a stateless design, every meaningful state transition is immediately persisted to Redis, PostgreSQL, S3, or a similar external store, so any worker can resume where a previous worker left off.
The principle is sometimes summarized as the
Twelve-Factor App's "processes are stateless and share-nothing"
rule — applied to the agentic AI context where "processes" are the worker pods that run your agent loop.

Multi-step agentic tasks routinely take minutes, hours, or days. A research agent may browse dozens of sources; a coding agent may iterate through a failing test suite over many LLM calls; a document-processing pipeline may batch thousands of files. During that window:
due to an OOM event, a bad LLM response, a network timeout, or a pod eviction by Kubernetes.
of the agent code, which terminates running processes.
— spinning up additional workers to process tasks in parallel.
an agent run hours later on a completely different machine.

If the agent keeps its state in process memory, every one of these events loses work and forces a full restart from the beginning. State externalization converts all of those events into recoverable incidents rather than failures.
A crashing worker does not lose progress. Any new worker that picks up the task reads the saved checkpoint and continues from where the previous worker stopped.

Because workers share no local state, you can run ten or a hundred of them concurrently against the same external stores. Each worker fetches the next available task, processes it, and writes results back — no coordination code required.
Rolling restarts replace old workers with new ones. In-flight tasks are re-queued and handled by new workers without restarting from scratch.
A long-running agent conversation that started on worker A can be resumed on worker B, or even resumed from a CLI on a developer's laptop, because the full context is in an external store.

State in an external store can be inspected, queried, and visualized independently of the agent process. Operations teams can examine current task state without attaching a debugger to a live process.
Independent scaling of compute and storage:
Workers (CPU-bound) and state stores (I/O-bound) scale on separate dimensions. Add workers to process more tasks; upgrade the database instance to handle larger state objects.

Every state read or write now involves a network round-trip. A Redis read from the same availability zone takes ~0.5 ms; a PostgreSQL query under load may take 5–20 ms. In tight loops that call the store dozens of times per LLM step, this adds up.

You must provision, monitor, back up, and scale the external stores. A single agent process becomes dependent on Redis, a relational database, and potentially an object store — each with its own failure mode.

State that lives in memory as Python objects must be serialized (JSON, msgpack, pickle) before writing and deserialized after reading. Large conversation histories or embedding arrays can make serialization a bottleneck.

When multiple workers read and update overlapping state (e.g., a shared task counter), you need atomic operations or distributed locking to prevent race conditions. These are solvable but require deliberate design.

A stateless worker that picks up a task must reconstruct its full working context by reading from the store. For complex agents with large conversation histories, this cold-start read can take significant time compared to an in-process agent that already has the data in RAM.
At the start of each agent step, the worker:
from the message queue — a small JSON object containing the task ID, session ID, and any inputs.
(conversation history, tool outputs, progress markers) from the external store using the session ID as the key.
Runs the agent logic: calls the LLM, executes tool calls, processes results.
back to the external store atomically — conversation history, new tool outputs, and the next step's progress marker.
Acknowledges the message queue entry (removing it from the queue), then either picks up the next task or enqueues a follow-up task for the next agent step.
If the worker crashes at step 3 or 4, the message queue's
ensures the task re-appears and is retried by a different worker. The external store may have a partial write from a crashed worker, so idempotent writes (e.g., using

attrs.alt: Architecture diagram: Stateless Agent Workers on the left read and write to external state stores on the right (Redis, PostgreSQL, S3, Vector DB, Message Queue)

content.text: The moment you run more than one agent worker concurrently, shared external state is the correct model.
Tasks that span multiple LLM calls benefit immediately from crash recovery via externalized checkpoints.
Tasks that require human review or approval:
The agent pauses mid-execution, persists its state, and resumes hours later when the human approves — impossible with in-process state.
Functions-as-a-service (AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Run) are inherently stateless. Externalized state is not optional here — the runtime has no persistent memory.
Isolate each tenant's state under a tenant-scoped key prefix or separate database schema.
Consider keeping state in-process only for single-step, short-lived agent calls (< 5 seconds, no recovery needed) where the overhead of external store round-trips would dominate the task time.
Identify every piece of data the agent needs across steps. Common fields:
Use Redis for ephemeral session state with a TTL; PostgreSQL for durable task records and audit logs; S3/GCS for large artifacts; a vector DB for semantic memory.
Every agent run gets a UUID. All state for that run is stored under keys prefixed with that UUID: e.g.,
Implement state read at task start:
The first thing a worker does after dequeuing a task is to fetch the current state snapshot. Abstract this behind a
Implement atomic state write after each step:
After the LLM call and tool execution, write the updated state before acknowledging the queue message. Use transactions or conditional writes to prevent partial updates.
Add distributed locking for concurrent agents:
When multiple sub-agents may update the same state concurrently, acquire a short-lived Redis lock before reading and release it after writing.
Prevent unbounded growth of the state store by expiring session keys after a reasonable inactivity window (e.g., 7 days for Redis sessions, 90 days for archived PostgreSQL records).
Deliberately kill a worker mid-task and confirm a new worker picks up the task, reads the persisted state, and continues correctly.

import redis
import json
from uuid import uuid4

r = redis.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, decode_responses=True)

SESSION_TTL = 60 * 60 * 24 * 7  # 7 days in seconds

def create_session(user_id: str) -> str:
    """Create a new agent session, persisted in Redis."""
    session_id = str(uuid4())
    state = {
        "session_id": session_id,
        "user_id": user_id,
        "messages": [],
        "step_index": 0,
        "status": "pending",
    }
    r.set(f"session:{session_id}:state", json.dumps(state), ex=SESSION_TTL)
    return session_id

def load_session(session_id: str) -> dict:
    """Load agent session state from Redis."""
    raw = r.get(f"session:{session_id}:state")
    if raw is None:
        raise KeyError(f"Session {session_id} not found (expired or never created)")
    return json.loads(raw)

def save_session(state: dict) -> None:
    """Persist updated state back to Redis, refreshing the TTL."""
    session_id = state["session_id"]
    r.set(f"session:{session_id}:state", json.dumps(state), ex=SESSION_TTL)

def append_message(session_id: str, role: str, content: str) -> None:
    """Atomically append a message to the session history using a Redis list."""
    msg = json.dumps({"role": role, "content": content})
    pipe = r.pipeline()
    pipe.rpush(f"session:{session_id}:messages", msg)
    pipe.expire(f"session:{session_id}:messages", SESSION_TTL)
    pipe.execute()

def get_messages(session_id: str) -> list[dict]:
    raw_list = r.lrange(f"session:{session_id}:messages", 0, -1)
    return [json.loads(m) for m in raw_list]

import psycopg2
import json
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from uuid import uuid4

conn = psycopg2.connect("postgresql://user:pass@localhost/agentdb")

def init_schema():
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute("""
            CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS agent_tasks (
                session_id  TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
                user_id     TEXT NOT NULL,
                status      TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',
                step_index  INT  NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
                state_json  JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}'::jsonb,
                created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
                updated_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
            )
        """)
    conn.commit()

def create_task(user_id: str) -> str:
    session_id = str(uuid4())
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute(
            "INSERT INTO agent_tasks (session_id, user_id, state_json) VALUES (%s, %s, %s)",
            (session_id, user_id, json.dumps({"messages": [], "tool_outputs": []}))
        )
    conn.commit()
    return session_id

def load_task(session_id: str) -> dict:
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute(
            "SELECT status, step_index, state_json FROM agent_tasks WHERE session_id = %s",
            (session_id,)
        )
        row = cur.fetchone()
    if row is None:
        raise KeyError(f"Task {session_id} not found")
    status, step_index, state_json = row
    return {"status": status, "step_index": step_index, **state_json}

def advance_task(session_id: str, new_state: dict, new_status: str = "running") -> None:
    """Update task state atomically. Uses optimistic step_index check to detect races."""
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute("""
            UPDATE agent_tasks
               SET status     = %s,
                   step_index = step_index + 1,
                   state_json = %s,
                   updated_at = now()
             WHERE session_id = %s
        """, (new_status, json.dumps(new_state), session_id))
    conn.commit()

import redis
import time
from contextlib import contextmanager

r = redis.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, decode_responses=True)

@contextmanager
def acquire_lock(resource: str, timeout: float = 5.0, ttl: int = 30):
    """
    Acquire a Redis-backed distributed lock on `resource`.
    Raises TimeoutError if the lock cannot be acquired within `timeout` seconds.
    Automatically releases the lock on exit (even after exceptions).
    """
    lock_key = f"lock:{resource}"
    lock_value = str(id(object()))  # unique per call
    deadline = time.monotonic() + timeout

    while time.monotonic() < deadline:
        # NX = only set if not exists; EX = expire in `ttl` seconds
        acquired = r.set(lock_key, lock_value, nx=True, ex=ttl)
        if acquired:
            try:
                yield
            finally:
                # Only delete if we still own the lock (guard against TTL expiry + re-acquisition)
                current = r.get(lock_key)
                if current == lock_value:
                    r.delete(lock_key)
            return
        time.sleep(0.05)

    raise TimeoutError(f"Could not acquire lock on {resource!r} within {timeout}s")

# Usage: safe concurrent update of shared agent state
def update_shared_counter(session_id: str, delta: int) -> int:
    with acquire_lock(f"session:{session_id}:counter"):
        raw = r.get(f"session:{session_id}:counter") or "0"
        new_val = int(raw) + delta
        r.set(f"session:{session_id}:counter", new_val, ex=86400)
    return new_val

receives a user question, fans out to a web-search tool, synthesizes findings over three LLM calls, and produces a final cited report. The task takes 45–90 seconds and involves sequential steps that must survive worker restarts.

import anthropic
import redis
import json
from uuid import uuid4

r = redis.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, decode_responses=True)
client = anthropic.Anthropic()
SESSION_TTL = 3600  # 1 hour

def save(session_id: str, state: dict) -> None:
    r.set(f"research:{session_id}", json.dumps(state), ex=SESSION_TTL)

def load(session_id: str) -> dict:
    raw = r.get(f"research:{session_id}")
    return json.loads(raw) if raw else {}

def run_research_agent(question: str, session_id: str | None = None) -> str:
    """
    A stateless research agent. Pass an existing session_id to resume a
    partially completed run after a crash. Start fresh by omitting it.
    """
    session_id = session_id or str(uuid4())
    state = load(session_id) or {
        "question": question,
        "step": "search",
        "search_results": None,
        "outline": None,
        "report": None,
    }

    # Step 1: search (idempotent — skip if already done)
    if state["step"] == "search":
        # In a real agent this would call a search tool; simulated here
        state["search_results"] = f"[search results for: {question}]"
        state["step"] = "outline"
        save(session_id, state)          # checkpoint

    # Step 2: generate outline
    if state["step"] == "outline":
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
            max_tokens=512,
            messages=[{
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"Create a 3-point outline for a report on: {state['question']}\n"
                    f"Use these search results: {state['search_results']}"
                ),
            }],
        )
        state["outline"] = resp.content[0].text
        state["step"] = "report"
        save(session_id, state)          # checkpoint

    # Step 3: write report
    if state["step"] == "report":
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
            max_tokens=1024,
            messages=[{
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"Write a concise research report following this outline:\n{state['outline']}\n"
                    f"Original question: {state['question']}"
                ),
            }],
        )
        state["report"] = resp.content[0].text
        state["step"] = "done"
        save(session_id, state)          # final checkpoint

    return state["report"]

# Worker A starts the task
session = str(uuid4())
print(f"Starting session {session}")

# Simulate crash after step 1 by calling with the same session_id
# Worker B picks up and resumes from the saved checkpoint
result = run_research_agent("What is state externalization?", session_id=session)
print(result[:200])
Storing state in a global variable or class attribute:
works fine for a single process, but a second worker instance starts with an empty list. Always load state from the external store at task start.
Writing state only at the end of a multi-step task means a crash at step 4 of 5 forces a full restart. Checkpoint after every meaningful step.
Forgetting to acknowledge the queue message:

If a worker reads a message but crashes before acknowledging, the message re-appears and gets retried — correct behavior. But if the worker succeeds and then crashes before acknowledging, the same task runs twice. Use at-least-once semantics and make your state writes idempotent.
If a retry causes the same step to run twice, an
will fail with a unique constraint violation. Use
Without expiry, abandoned sessions accumulate indefinitely. Set a reasonable TTL on Redis keys. Archive completed tasks in PostgreSQL and delete from Redis.
API keys or tokens written into the session state end up in Redis or PostgreSQL in plaintext. Store credential references (e.g., a secret ID) and resolve them at runtime from a secrets manager.
Serializing a full 100-message conversation history on every step is wasteful. Keep the immutable conversation history in a Redis list and append-only, rather than rewriting the entire structure.
Define a dataclass or Pydantic model for your session state. Serialize with
. Schema evolution is easier when you have a versioned model.
Namespace all keys with a prefix and session ID:
prevents accidental collisions between environments and tenants.
Store the current step's working data in Redis (low latency); archive completed task records to PostgreSQL (queryable, durable). Don't put 90-day archives in Redis.
field on every write. Read-modify-write only succeeds if the version matches what you read. Retry on conflict.
Prefer append-only patterns for conversation history:
Use a Redis list or PostgreSQL append-only table for message history rather than deserializing, appending, and re-serializing the entire list on every step.
Write integration tests that interrupt a task at each step and verify that resuming from the saved state produces the same final output.
Each worker should maintain a persistent connection pool to Redis and PostgreSQL rather than opening new connections per task. Use
Batch multiple Redis reads/writes in a single round-trip using
. A common pattern is to pipeline the TTL refresh, state write, and counter increment in one call.
Route read-only queries (state reads, history lookups) to a replica to offload the primary node. Use
If serialized state exceeds a few kilobytes, apply
compression before storing in Redis. Decompression at read time is fast and reduces Redis memory usage significantly.

Keep at most the last N messages (e.g., 20) in Redis hot state and archive older messages to S3. The LLM context window is bounded anyway, so storing an unbounded history is both expensive and unused.
Redis Cluster and PostgreSQL partitioning both scale linearly when data is distributed by a hash of the session ID. Design your key scheme to enable sharding from the start.
Sub-ms latency; data lost on restart unless persistence enabled
ACID; JSONB column for flexible state; full SQL for queries
litestream streams WAL to S3 for durability
Pay-per-request; single-table design for session state
Cheap at scale; read-after-write consistent in S3
Kafka: durable, ordered; SQS: managed, at-least-once
Built into Redis; consumer groups for competing workers
HTTP API option avoids persistent TCP connections

State externalization is the middle ground: significantly better fault tolerance and scalability than in-process state, without the full programming model change required by durable execution frameworks.

Enable encryption-at-rest for Redis (via Redis Enterprise or Upstash) and PostgreSQL (via Transparent Data Encryption or disk-level encryption). Store S3 artifacts with server-side encryption (AES-256 or SSE-KMS).
Use TLS for all connections to Redis (
). Never pass credentials or conversation content over plaintext connections.
Use Redis ACLs to limit each service to its own key prefix. A worker that only needs session state should not be able to read administrative keys or other tenants' data.

Never write API keys, OAuth tokens, or passwords into Redis or PostgreSQL session state. Store a reference (a secret name or ARN) and resolve it from AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault at runtime.
Implement circuit breakers for store failures:
If Redis becomes unavailable, a worker should not silently continue with stale in-memory state. Use a circuit breaker (e.g.,
) that fast-fails state operations when the store is degraded, triggering task re-queue rather than silent data loss.
Deserialize state through a Pydantic model on every load. Reject or repair state that fails validation rather than propagating corrupt data through the agent loop.
Monitor store health alongside agent health:
Alert on Redis memory usage, replication lag, and connection pool exhaustion. A saturated Redis instance causes silent state-write failures that are worse than an explicit agent crash.
holds no persistent data in process memory — all state that must survive a step boundary lives in an external store.
Choose the right store for each data class:
for low-latency ephemeral session data and locks;
— not just at the end of the task — so that a crash at any point forces at most one step to be retried, not the entire run.
using upsert semantics and conditional updates. Retries are inherent in queue-based systems; non-idempotent writes turn retries into corruption.
(Redis SETNX / Redlock) when multiple concurrent sub-agents read and update overlapping state. Lock scope should be as narrow as possible — lock the specific resource, not the entire session.
and archive completed task records to cold storage. External state stores are not infinite; unbounded growth degrades latency for all tenants.
in session state. Store references; resolve at runtime from a secrets manager.
State externalization occupies the practical sweet spot between the simplicity of in-process state and the full recovery guarantees of a durable execution engine like Temporal.
